---
title: "3.7 Class Methods"
subtitle: "Class Variables and Methods"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Class Variables and Methods

### Topic intro

In Unit 1, we learned about methods by using the `Math` class and its many **static** methods like `Math.random()`.

We also wrote our own `main` methods and other methods that were static.

These are called **class methods** because they belong to the class and not to any object of the class.

In this lesson, you will learn to write your own class variables and methods using the keyword `static` and compare them to instance variables and methods.

## Class Methods

### Belong to the class

Class methods belong to the class and not to any object of the class.

They are called with the class name and the dot operator:

```java
ClassName.methodName();
Math.random();
```

## One Copy for the Class

### Static method call forms

There is only one copy of a class variable or method for the whole class.

For example, the `main` method is a class method because there should only be one `main` method.

If the method is in the same class, you can call it from other static methods in the class:

```java
ClassName.methodName();
methodName();
```

## Calling Class Methods

### Source pattern

```java
// Calling class methods
// ClassName.methodName();
int x = Math.sqrt(9);

// If the method is in the same class,
// you can call it with or without the class name
// from other static methods in the class
ClassName.methodName();
methodName();
```

## Comparing Method Calls {.image-fit}

### Static, instance, and same-class calls

![Comparing Method Calls to Static and Instance Methods](../../assets/3-class-creation/3-7-class-variables-and-methods/FlowChartCallingMethods.png){fig-align="center" width="68%"}

Class methods are called using the class name.

Instance methods can only be called using an object of the class.

## Method Call Rules

### Where the call happens

If you are calling an instance method from the `main` method or from another class, you must first create an object of that class and then call its methods using `object.methodName()`.

If you are calling the method from within the same class, you can just call `methodName()`.

```java
object.methodName();  // from main or another class
methodName();         // from within the same class
```

That call will refer to the current object.

## Static Declarations

### Where `static` is placed

When writing class methods, they can be `public` or `private`.

The `static` keyword is placed right after the public/private modifier and right before the type of variables and methods in their declarations.

```java
public class ClassName
{
    // static class variable
    public static type variableName;

    // static class method
    public static returnType methodName(parameters)
    {
        // implementation not shown
    }
}
```

## Static Access Rule

### What class methods can access

Class methods can access or change the values of class variables and can call other class methods.

However, they cannot access or change the values of non-static instance variables or call non-static instance methods without being passed an instance of the class via a parameter.

For example, the `main` method can call other static methods directly with or without the class name, but it cannot call instance methods without first creating an object.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: staticmethods`

Note that the static method `printStatic` has errors in it because it cannot access the instance variables and methods.

Fix the `printStatic` method by giving it the `Person` object as a parameter and using this object to call its instance variables and methods.

Also fix the method call to `printStatic` in `main` to pass in the object as an argument.

## staticmethods Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Person
{
    // instance variables
    private String name;
    private String email;

    // constructor
    public Person(String initName, String initEmail)
    {
        name = initName;
        email = initEmail;
    }

    // instance method
    public void print()
    {
        System.out.println(name + ": " + email);
    }

    // static method
    public static void printStatic()
    {
        System.out.println("Static methods cannot print out non-static variables.");
        // The following lines will cause errors

        // TODO: Fix this by giving it the Person object as a parameter
        // Use the object to print out the name and call its instance methods.
        System.out.println(name);
        print();
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        Person p = new Person("Jana", "jana@gmail.com");

        // Call p's instance method to print
        p.print();

        // Call printStatic with the class name
        // Note this has errors.
        // TODO: Fix the errors by giving it the object p as an argument
        Person.printStatic();
    }
}
```
:::

## Focused Fix

### Pass an object into the static method

The source task asks students to change the method so a `Person` object is passed into `printStatic`.

The static method can then use that object to access the instance data and instance method.

The call in `main` must also pass the object `p`.

## Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The tests check for:

::: {.tight-list}
- a call containing `printStatic(p)`
- a method header containing `printStatic(Person`
:::

The classroom trace focus is why the original `printStatic` produced a static-context error.

## After the Exercise

### Static methods as utilities

This example also shows how instance methods easily share data variables without the need for parameters.

Static methods cannot directly access the instance methods.

They are often used for utility methods, like `Math` operations, that do not need access to the instance variables of an object.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- explain why `Math.random()` and `main` are class methods
- call a class method with `ClassName.methodName()`
- distinguish class-method calls from instance-method calls
- place `static` correctly in a method or variable declaration
- explain the static-context error in `printStatic`
- fix `printStatic` by passing a `Person` object as a parameter
:::

## End

### 3.7 Part 1 complete

Next: class variables and static counters.
